description: From total catastrophe, anything at all.

guides.text: The goal is $\bot \to A$. In words: if falsity holds, then $A$ is true. This sounds absurd — but that's the point. Classical logic takes the position that from a [contradiction|contradiction], **anything** follows. This is called *[ex-falso|ex falso quodlibet]* — 'from falsehood, whatever you please'. Its dual is $\top$ (top, truth): from anything, truth follows. We'll prove that soon.
Assume the antecedent. The [formula|formula] for falsity is `_|_` — the engine also accepts `bottom` or `false`.

The rule `ex-falso` takes two arguments: a step number whose [conclusion|conclusion] is $\bot$, and a [formula|formula] (any formula) you want to derive. We have $\bot$ at step 1 — let's pull $A$ out of thin air.
Discharge the [assumption|assumption]. From $\bot$, anything follows — and we've proved it.

unlocks.name: ex-falso
unlocks.description: From a contradiction ($\bot$), you can derive any formula $p$. The principle of explosion — *ex falso quodlibet*.
